model_summary: Initial gVAR-ready observation model tailored to a sleep-maintenance complaint with early-morning awakening and subsequent daytime impairment. Six criteria capture nightly events (C01–C06) centered on waking at ~04:30, sleep initiation, arousal, in-bed doomscrolling, distress/rumination, and lunch-time functioning. Six parsimonious, modifiable predictors (P01–P06) target circadian entrainment (morning light), evening screen exposure, pre-sleep cognitive load, bedtime routine adherence, sleep-environment quality, and daytime energy. Measurements combine end-of-day and momentary EMA with a passive-signal (light) stream to maximize within-person variance while maintaining feasibility. A 14-day window with 4 momentary EMA probes/day plus a daily diary provides estimable, non-redundant signals suitable for initial gVAR modeling. The plan preserves the complaint’s resolution trajectory and supports iterated predictor refinement as data accrue.
variable_selection_notes: Selected criteria focus on the nocturnal onset pattern, nocturnal cognitive/behavioral arousal, in-bed engagement, morning rumination, and daytime impairment. Predictors emphasize modifiable salutogenic levers with high within-person variance potential: circadian entrainment (P01), evening screen behavior (P02), pre-sleep cognitive load (P03), sleep routines (P04), sleep environment (P05), and daytime energy (P06). To minimize redundancy, each predictor targets a distinct mechanism; no overlapping self-regulation constructs beyond necessary control variables. Some predictors (P01) leverage passive data (light exposure) to complement self-reports. The recall windows emphasize “since last prompt” for momentary items and “past night” for sleep-night composites. Stationarity and nonlinearity considerations are noted in gVAR-handling hints; nonlinear effects are acknowledged in the predicted_signs/expected_effects where appropriate.

momentary_ema.notes: Prompts distributed across daytime blocks with slight jitter to reduce predictability; avoid sleep windows.

daily_diary.notes: End-of-day diary capturing last-night sleep and current-day functioning.

passive_streams.notes: Passive signals (morning light, environment proxies) complement EMA signals.

design_recommendations.gvar_readiness_notes: Atomic criteria with distinct mechanisms; passive signal augments self-reports; variance is expected to be adequate for gVAR estimation.
design_recommendations.variance_support_notes: Prefer 1–9 Likert scales for EMA measures to maximize within-person variance; apply within-person standardization (z-scoring) where appropriate.
design_recommendations.missingness_risk_notes: Plan reminders; allow small windows for late responses; document missingness with a brief follow-up.

safety_notes: Not a diagnosis. Do not provide medication dosing. If acute risk is identified, seek professional help.

criteria_variables.label: Early-morning awakening (sleep maintenance)

measurement.item_or_signal: On the last night, how much did you wake around 04:30 and have trouble maintaining sleep? Rate from 1 (not at all) to 9 (severe) on your usual night.
measurement.notes: Captures sleep-maintenance difficulty around the reported wake time.

criteria_variables.mapped_rationale: Best-matching leaf: 'sleep-maintenance insomnia / early-morning awakening' with leaf 'early_morning_awakening'. The variable label 'Early-morning awakening (sleep maintenance)' and measurement: end-of-night EMA self-report on last night (04:30 wake, 1-9), recalls 'past_night', aligns with early-morning awakening as a sleep-maintenance difficulty; the candidate is the most specific operational construct among leaves, explicitly modeling early-morning awakenings within chronic insomnia spectrum. Other leaves are either broader (unspecified insomnia) or focus on nighttime arousals without explicit morning awakening or are about broader circadian issues; thus 26355 is most specific and matches mechanism of nocturnal arousal leading to morning impairment.

measurement.notes: Assesses sleep-onset success, a core insomnia-shaped symptom.

predictor_variables.label: Morning light exposure (circadian entrainment)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Morning light strengthens circadian entrainment, promotes wakefulness during the day, and reduces nocturnal arousal that contributes to early awakenings.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: S01: morning outdoor light; S02: safe light-therapy device usage

measurement.item_or_signal: Ambient light exposure in the 60 minutes after waking (lux)
measurement.notes: Utilizes device sensors to quantify circadian entrainment signal strength.

expected_effects.rationale: Increased daylight exposure after waking is expected to improve sleep maintenance.
Daylight cues can support earlier sleep initiation when combined with stable routines.
Strong morning light may reduce early-morning arousal; effect is moderating.
Direct link to doomscroll engagement is uncertain from morning light alone.
Indirect mood/rumination pathways not fully specified here.
Enhanced circadian stability supports daytime functioning.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Best-matching leaf: MORNING BRIGHT LIGHT EXPOSURE. The candidate leaf 6331 is BIO / sleep_circadian_and_restoration / circadian_entrainment / light_cues / morning_bright_light_exposure, which precisely aligns with using morning light as a circadian entrainment cue and uses the same measurement modality and signal type. Reduced pre-sleep stimulation supports smoother sleep onset.
Lower pre-sleep arousal with less stimulation may reduce awakening arousal.
Direct link to in-bed doomscrolling is uncertain.
Less distress after evening screen use may reduce morning rumination.
Reduced late-evening stimulation can improve daytime energy.

measurement.item_or_signal: On the last night, how much did you wake around 04:30 and have trouble maintaining sleep? Rate from 1 (not at all) to 9 (severe) on your usual night.
measurement.notes: Captures sleep-maintenance difficulty around the reported wake time.

criteria.label: Sleep onset ability
criteria.query_text_used: Sleep onset ability | Operational item/signal: Did you fall asleep at your intended bedtime with minimal difficulty yesterday? Rate 1-9 on how easily you initiated sleep. | Polarity: higher_is_better | Notes: Assesses sleep-onset success, a core insomnia-shaped symptom.

mapping.rationale: Variable label: Sleep onset ability. Measurement item: 'Did you fall asleep at your intended bedtime with minimal difficulty yesterday? Rate 1-9 on how easily you initiated sleep.' This exact item aligns with subjective sleep-onset latency (latency to fall asleep) and maps to the leaf 'subjective_sleep_onset_latency_report' under insomnia-related disorders. It is more specific to latency/initiating sleep than broader difficulty_in_sleep_onset or other onset/cognition leaves, making it the best operational match for the given EMA self-report item. Pseudoprofile context supports nocturnal-onset symptoms (C02) being captured via end-of-day/latency-focused latency measures, consistent with the six nocturnal criteria.

measurement.item_or_signal: Did you fall asleep at your intended bedtime with minimal difficulty yesterday? Rate 1-9 on how easily you initiated sleep.
measurement.notes: Assesses sleep-onset success, a core insomnia-shaped symptom.

criteria.label: Immediate cognitive/physiological arousal on awakening
criteria.query_text_used: Immediate cognitive/physiological arousal on awakening | Operational item/signal: Upon waking around 04:30 today, rate your mental activation right after waking (0-9). | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Captures rapid cognitive arousal immediately after awakening.

mapping.rationale: Variable is Immediate cognitive/physiological arousal on awakening measured as momentary EMA right after awakening (~04:30). The most specific operational construct among candidates is 'morning_hyperarousal_signs_post_awakening' (clinical insomnia/early-morning arousal phenotype), which directly represents post-awakening arousal (cognitive activation and physiological arousal) during the morning window. This leaf captures the mechanism (circadian- and sleep-maintenance related morning hyperarousal) and uses the same 0-9 scale and momentary EMA approach, aligning with the variable's label, measurement mode, and recall window.

measurement.item_or_signal: Upon waking around 04:30 today, rate your mental activation right after waking (0-9).
measurement.notes: Captures rapid cognitive arousal immediately after awakening.

criteria.label: In-bed/device-mediated engagement during attempted rest
criteria.query_text_used: In-bed/device-mediated engagement during attempted rest | Operational item/signal: During the attempted rest after awakening, how much did you engage with a device/content (doomscroll) instead of passive rest? Rate 1-9. | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Assesses passive vs. active engagement during wake windows.

mapping.rationale: Variable label 'In-bed/device-mediated engagement during attempted rest' corresponds to an automatic or goal-directed behavior during wakefulness in bed (doomscrolling) after awakening. The candidate leaf 26205 'automatic_behaviors_during_wakefulness' precisely encodes automatic behaviors during wakefulness and aligns with the measurement item (device engagement during wake in bed) and the 'since last prompt' EMA context. It is more specific to the behavioral engagement with devices in bed than other leaves (e.g., bed-exit, parasomnias) and captures a mechanism affecting sleep maintenance circadian entrainment.

measurement.item_or_signal: During the attempted rest after awakening, how much did you engage with a device/content (doomscroll) instead of passive rest? Rate 1-9.
measurement.notes: Assesses passive vs. active engagement during wake windows.

criteria.label: Exposure to distressing/ruminative content
criteria.query_text_used: Exposure to distressing/ruminative content | Operational item/signal: During early-morning awakenings, rate the extent of doomscrolling-related distress or rumination (1-9). | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Captures cognitive-affective load from online content after waking.

mapping.rationale: The variable maps to post-wake rumination/distress triggered by doomscrolling after early-morning awakening. The closest, most specific CRITERION leaf is 'intrusive_rumination_post_awakening' (sleep-wake disorders > insomnia > early-morning awakening) which captures intrusive rumination after waking, matching the momentary EMA item rating (1-9) of doomscrolling-related distress. Other candidates are more generic or broader (e.g., rumination distress_level, frequency) or relate to sleep disturbances unrelated to the wake context; thus this leaf best operationalizes the variable in context.
mapping.leaf_embed_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / sleep-wake disorders / insomnia disorders / chronic insomnia disorder / early-morning awakening insomnia / *intrusive_rumination_post_awakening
mapping.leaf_full_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / sleep-wake disorders / insomnia disorders / chronic insomnia disorder / early-morning awakening insomnia / intrusive_rumination_post_awakening

measurement.notes: Captures cognitive-affective load from online content after waking.

criteria.label: Daytime functional impairment by midday
criteria.query_text_used: Daytime functional impairment by midday | Operational item/signal: By lunchtime today, rate your daytime functioning compared to a typical day (1-9). | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Links nocturnal disturbance to daytime impairment.

mapping.rationale: Variable C06 is 'Daytime functional impairment by midday' measured via end-of-day EMA. The leaf 26250 corresponds to 'impaired_daytime_functioning' under insomnia disorders (unspecified insomnia), providing a direct, highly specific operational leaf for daytime impairment resulting from nocturnal disturbance. This matches both the label (daytime impairment) and the end-of-day measurement type, aligning with the pseudoprofile's focus on daytime functioning after nocturnal events.

measurement.item_or_signal: By lunchtime today, rate your daytime functioning compared to a typical day (1-9).
measurement.notes: Links nocturnal disturbance to daytime impairment.

predictors.label: Morning light exposure (circadian entrainment)
predictors.query_text_used: Morning light exposure (circadian entrainment) | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Morning light strengthens circadian entrainment, promotes wakefulness during the day, and reduces nocturnal arousal that contributes to early awakenings. | Intervention examples: S01: morning outdoor light; S02: safe light-therapy device usage | Measurement: passive_phone_sensor | Ambient light exposure in the 60 minutes after waking (lux) | Prior ontology hint: [BIO] Sleep_and_Circadian > Circadian_Entrainment

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf: MORNING BRIGHT LIGHT EXPOSURE. The variable P01 is Morning light exposure (circadian entrainment) with measurement via passive phone sensor, capturing ambient light exposure in the 60 minutes after waking (lux). The candidate leaf 6331 is BIO / sleep_circadian_and_restoration / circadian_entrainment / light_cues / morning_bright_light_exposure, which precisely aligns with using morning light as a circadian entrainment cue and uses the same measurement modality and signal type. Other leaves describe daytime or evening light or broader daylight exposure, not the specific morning post-wake light cue targeted by P01.

source_variable.label: Morning light exposure (circadian entrainment)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Morning light strengthens circadian entrainment, promotes wakefulness during the day, and reduces nocturnal arousal that contributes to early awakenings.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: S01: morning outdoor light; S02: safe light-therapy device usage

measurement.item_or_signal: Ambient light exposure in the 60 minutes after waking (lux)
measurement.notes: Utilizes device sensors to quantify circadian entrainment signal strength.

expected_effects.rationale: Increased daylight exposure after waking is expected to improve sleep maintenance.
Daylight cues can support earlier sleep initiation when combined with stable routines.
Strong morning light may reduce early-morning arousal; effect is moderating.
Direct link to doomscroll engagement is uncertain from morning light alone.
Indirect mood/rumination pathways not fully specified here.
Enhanced circadian stability supports daytime functioning.

predictors.label: Evening screen exposure before bed
predictors.query_text_used: Evening screen exposure before bed | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Evening screen exposure can shift sleep pressure and circadian timing, potentially increasing wakefulness and arousal at night. | Intervention examples: S01: screen-curfew; S02: use of blue-light filters; S03: alternative pre-sleep activities | Measurement: EMA_self_report | In the two hours before your planned bedtime, how stimulating was your screen use? Rate 1 (not stimulating) to 9 (highly stimulating). | Prior ontology hint: [PSYCHO] Sleep-related cognitive-behavioral interventions > conditioning_and_associations

mapping.rationale: Maps P02 'Evening screen exposure before bed' to a circadian entrainment leaf that directly encodes late-evening screen exposure: 'late_evening_screen_reduction' (BIO / sleep_circadian_and_restoration / circadian_entrainment / light_cues / late_evening_screen_reduction). This aligns with the momentary EMA item measuring how stimulating screen use is in the two hours before bedtime and reflects the mechanism that screen exposure can shift sleep pressure and circadian timing, increasing nocturnal arousal. Among candidates, this is the most specific construct for the predictor variable.

source_variable.label: Evening screen exposure before bed
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Evening screen exposure can shift sleep pressure and circadian timing, potentially increasing wakefulness and arousal at night.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: S01: screen-curfew; S02: use of blue-light filters; S03: alternative pre-sleep activities

measurement.item_or_signal: In the two hours before your planned bedtime, how stimulating was your screen use? Rate 1 (not stimulating) to 9 (highly stimulating).
measurement.notes: Captures pre-sleep cognitive arousal tendencies.

expected_effects.rationale: Evening screen curtailment improves sleep maintenance if implemented consistently.
Reduced pre-sleep stimulation supports smoother sleep onset.
Lower pre-sleep arousal with less stimulation may reduce awakening arousal.
Direct link to in-bed doomscrolling is uncertain.
Less distress after evening screen use may reduce morning rumination.
Reduced late-evening stimulation can improve daytime energy.

predictors.label: Pre-sleep cognitive load and rumination
predictors.query_text_used: Pre-sleep cognitive load and rumination | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Higher pre-sleep cognitive load and rumination prolong sleep latency and elevate nocturnal arousal. | Intervention examples: S01: narrative detachment and unloading; S02: worry management strategies | Measurement: EMA_self_report | In the last hour before bed, rate your cognitive load and rumination about worries on a 1-9 scale. | Prior ontology hint: [PSYCHO] Sleep-related cognitive-behavioral interventions > cognitive_work

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf: presleep_detachment_routine_design_psychological (PSYCHO / cognitive_behavioral_therapies / sleep_related_cognitive_behavioral_interventions / presleep_mental_skills / detachment_and_unloading / presleep_detachment_routine_design_psychological). It directly targets pre-sleep cognitive load and rumination by designing a detachment/unloading routine before sleep, aligning with the variable label Pre-sleep cognitive load and rumination and the EMA item capturing cognitive load and worries in the hour before bed.

source_variable.label: Pre-sleep cognitive load and rumination
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Higher pre-sleep cognitive load and rumination prolong sleep latency and elevate nocturnal arousal.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: S01: narrative detachment and unloading; S02: worry management strategies

measurement.item_or_signal: In the last hour before bed, rate your cognitive load and rumination about worries on a 1-9 scale.
measurement.notes: Captures cognitive load and rumination pre-sleep.

expected_effects.rationale: Lower pre-sleep cognitive load may improve sleep maintenance.
Lower cognitive load can ease sleep onset.
Reducing cognitive arousal at night may lower awakening arousal.
Less cognitive engagement may reduce late-night doomscroll risk.
Lower rumination reduces morning distress.
Lower pre-sleep cognitive load supports better daytime energy.

predictors.label: Sleep routine adherence (stimulus-control bed setup)
predictors.query_text_used: Sleep routine adherence (stimulus-control bed setup) | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Consistent sleep routines (stimulus control) reduce sleep latency and wake after sleep onset, improving maintenance and daytime energy. | Intervention examples: S01: protocolized pre-sleep routine; S02: cue-control strategy | Measurement: EMA_self_report | How closely did you follow your planned pre-sleep routine tonight? Rate 1-9. | Prior ontology hint: [PSYCHO] Change_process_and_therapeutic_phases > behavioral_integration

mapping.rationale: Chosen leaf idx 2242 corresponds to PSYCHO / change_process_and_therapeutic_phases / behavior_change_theories_and_modern_protocol_arcs / modern_clinical_protocol_arcs_solutions / cbti_sleep_maintenance_plan_implementation. This variable is Sleep routine adherence (stimulus-control bed setup) measured via momentary EMA assessing how closely the user followed their planned pre-sleep routine. This aligns with implementing a sleep-maintenance plan (CBTI) and stimulus-control components, making cbti_sleep_maintenance_plan_implementation the most specific operational construct for this predictor.

source_variable.label: Sleep routine adherence (stimulus-control bed setup)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Consistent sleep routines (stimulus control) reduce sleep latency and wake after sleep onset, improving maintenance and daytime energy.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: S01: protocolized pre-sleep routine; S02: cue-control strategy

measurement.item_or_signal: How closely did you follow your planned pre-sleep routine tonight? Rate 1-9.
measurement.notes: Assesses adherence to stimulus-control behaviors.

expected_effects.rationale: Better adherence reduces risk of premature wake and maintenance problems.
Consistent routine supports sleep initiation.
Regular routines may reduce nocturnal arousal.
Less in-bed doomscrolling with a steady routine.
Lower post-sleep rumination due to structured routines.
Improved daytime energy with stable pre-sleep practices.

predictors.label: Bedroom sleep environment quality
predictors.query_text_used: Bedroom sleep environment quality | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: A dark, cool, quiet room improves sleep initiation and maintenance; improves circadian signaling and comfort. | Intervention examples: S01: blackout curtains; S02: white-noise or earplugs; S03: maintain cool room temperature | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Rate current sleep environment quality tonight (darkness, quiet, temperature) on a 1-9 scale. | Prior ontology hint: [BIO] Sleep_and_Circadian > Sleep_Environment

mapping.rationale: Variable P05 maps to bedroom sleep environment quality. The best leaf match among candidates is idx 4665: bedroom_thermal_zoning under indoor_sleep_comfort_environment. This leaf directly captures the thermal/ambient conditions of the bedroom that strongly influence sleep initiation and maintenance, aligning with the mechanism rationale (dark, cool, quiet room) and the measurement intent (momentary EMA rating of sleep environment). While the measurement item targets darkness, quiet, and temperature, and other leaves cover lighting or noise specifically, bedroom thermal zoning is the most specific operational construct available for a composite sleep-environment predictor within the Sleep_Environment domain, making it the best single-leaf match for P05 in this ontology set.

source_variable.label: Bedroom sleep environment quality
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: A dark, cool, quiet room improves sleep initiation and maintenance; improves circadian signaling and comfort.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: S01: blackout curtains; S02: white-noise or earplugs; S03: maintain cool room temperature

measurement.item_or_signal: Rate current sleep environment quality tonight (darkness, quiet, temperature) on a 1-9 scale.
measurement.notes: Captures environmental conditions related to sleep.

expected_effects.rationale: Better environment supports sleep maintenance.
Improved sleep onset with optimal room conditions.
Lower arousal with better environment.
Less doomscrolling tied to environment stability.
Calm environment reduces rumination risk.
Better bedroom quality supports daytime functioning.

predictors.label: Midday energy / daytime functioning cue
predictors.query_text_used: Midday energy / daytime functioning cue | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Daytime energy reflects circadian alignment and sleep quality; higher energy signals salutogenic functioning. | Intervention examples: S01: light exposure; S02: short aerobic activity | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Rate your energy level at lunchtime today on a 1-9 scale. | Prior ontology hint: [BIO] Sleep_and_Circadian > Sleep_Routines_and_Practices

mapping.rationale: Variable P06 corresponds to 'Midday energy / daytime functioning cue' measured as a momentary energy rating at lunchtime via EMA self-report on a smartphone. The closest leaf is the EMA-based 'momentary_cognition_and_energy / ema_mental_energy' (idx 4498), which captures instant energy as a daytime predictor mechanism aligned with circadian entrainment and daytime functioning. This leaf reflects the intended mechanism (daytime energy as downstream from sleep quality) and the measurement mode/type (momentary EMA self-report).

source_variable.label: Midday energy / daytime functioning cue
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Daytime energy reflects circadian alignment and sleep quality; higher energy signals salutogenic functioning.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: S01: light exposure; S02: short aerobic activity

measurement.item_or_signal: Rate your energy level at lunchtime today on a 1-9 scale.
measurement.notes: Assesses daytime energy as a downstream outcome of sleep processes.

expected_effects.rationale: Better nighttime sleep translates to higher daytime energy.
Smooth sleep onset can improve day performance.
Lower nocturnal arousal supports daytime energy.
Reduced night-time reactivity may improve next-day energy.
Less distress can improve daytime functioning.
Higher energy reflects salutogenic state.
